title: The Value of Experience — Good Will Hunting and What it Means to ‘Know’
text:
Image by Was a bee


After a recent re-watch of ‘Good Will Hunting’ — one of my favourite films — I was again struck by the remarkable conversation between troubled young janitor Will Hunting (Matt Damon), an initially unrecognised genius, and his therapist, Sean (Robin Williams), whom Will is forced to see as part of a deal to stay out of prison. In this specific scene, which takes place on a park bench overlooking a pond, Sean verbally reflects on Will’s intellectual prowess — his ability to critically process and recall staggering amounts of information seemingly without any difficulty — but also his tendency to use this ability to avoid opening up emotionally, thereby hurting himself and those close to him. This reflection follows from their previous meeting, in which Will arrogantly assumes to know Sean after one conversation and an analysis of a painting in the latter’s office — a personal affront that led to the therapist losing his temper.
Of course, the thought that one cannot truly know another is not unintuitive or particularly strange to us. It is often the case that there are substantial differences even in the way we experience the exact same phenomenon. For instance, think back to 2015 and the fervent debates surrounding the colour of the accursed dress pictured here (I know, simpler times). In this way, even if Will had, in fact, experienced the same things that Sean has, he would still not have been able to know Sean.



The Dress


Regardless of this, Sean’s point that the experience of something adds to the knowledge of that thing seems to me a clear homage to philosopher Frank Jackson’s famous 1982 article “Epiphenomenal Qualia”. In this (scary-sounding) article, Jackson introduces a thought experiment colloquially known as Mary’s Room — a popular thought experiment in undergraduate philosophy of mind courses, where I had the privilege of encountering it first.
The experiment asks that the reader imagine a brilliant neuroscientist, Mary (or a certain Will Hunting, for that matter), to exist permanently in a room devoid of colours other than black and white. In this room, Mary has access to say, a black and white version of the internet. Due to being a highly talented scientist and having access to all of the resources necessary for obtaining knowledge, Mary knows all there is to know of the accounts, descriptions and empirical evidence on colour. She knows which frequencies and intensities are associated with each colour in the visible spectrum, knows how the brain biologically processes these colours, and knows how other people have attempted to describe the experience of colour. However, and crucially, one must also imagine that Mary has never actually perceived colour herself. All she sees, and ever has seen, has been in black and white. The primary question the experiment requires us to ponder on is this: will Mary gain any new knowledge when she goes outside of this room; when she physically experiences seeing in colour for the first time?
According to Jackson, the answer to this question provides evidence either for or against the existence of qualia — the subjective and purely qualitative properties of experiences. If Mary learns something new about sunsets as soon as she looks at that first rust-coloured sunset, then it would be proof that experiencing something adds to the knowledge of that thing. This, in turn, would mean the falsity of physicalism — the metaphysical belief that everything in the world, all of reality, is physical in essence, and that physical explanations of things like consciousness and our mental states fully encompass the knowledge we can gather about these phenomena. Although this may sound intuitive to many of us, it is still a highly contested argument, one that I invite you to look into yourself.
Jackson’s argument encapsulates one of these contestations. He believes there is something other than the physical that can add value to what we consider knowledge — these subjective experiences. Even though Mary may know everything about the science of colour perception, she cannot know what the experience of blue is like if she has never seen blue, and therefore does not know everything there is to know about blue. Jackson contends that she has learned something new via this non-physical experience, and hence, that complete knowledge of something must require the subjective experience of that thing.
Let us reconsider once again the scene from Good Will Hunting. It seems that Sean, our therapist, is a proponent of the existence of qualia. He claims that because Will has never experienced being in the Sistine Chapel, has never smelled it or looked upon Michelangelo’s ceiling, he therefore can never know it in its entirety, regardless of whether he is a genius or not. Like Mary from the experiment, Will is living in a metaphorically ‘colourless’ world if he believes that his physical knowledge encapsulates the total amount of knowledge on any subject. Sean hopes that making this point proves to Will that his intellectual ability is not all that encompasses him, thereby encouraging him to open up and be honest about his actual lived-experience. This is not just interesting from the perspective of the philosophical value of experience, but naturally also from a psychological one. Regardless, I would highly recommend watching the scene, or better yet, the film itself, to fully appreciate the subtleties underlying many of the excellent dialogues in it.



Park Bench Scene from Good Will Hunting — Miramax Pictures

title: Football, Absurdity and Camus
text:
Albert Camus, Winner of the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957. Source


Recently — after a long injury-induced hiatus — I joined a group of friends for a friendly Friday afternoon game of football. After the session, and while I was nursing my aching old body back to health, I was reminded of the often undervalued role that the sport has had on my life. I’ve enjoyed many carefree moments whilst chasing after a ball, happily engaged in this activity. Similarly, I’ve spent countless hours staring at a television screen whilst 22 other blokes pass a round object about (although, as an Arsenal fan, these hours were often not particularly happy). This seems puzzling. What could be so fascinating about a bunch of people running around on a rectangular field of grass for 90 minutes whilst trying to kick an inflated sphere over some arbitrary chalked line, and then go absolutely berserk when achieving this? It seems decidedly devoid of any importance — absurd even. The absurdity is compounded when one considers the proportion of people who have dedicated their entire lives to this activity.
On the occasions that I’ve had to explain why football captures the imagination the way it does, I’ve always found it practically impossible. How does one explain the beauty of a top-corner volley, or the feeling of being crunched in a shocking studs-up tackle, or the absolute despondence of getting an injury and missing out on something that brings you happiness? It always felt like you had to get it. That’s why I don’t even try to explain why these days, except for asserting the ole “because I like it” conversation-ender. Luckily, however, in my post-game reflection, I recalled one who might help in explaining how to deal with the absurdity of football. One who was, coincidentally, also a fan of the beautiful game.
Naturally, our guide is Albert Camus, the famous existentialist philosopher (although he would object to both these descriptors). Camus had an interesting relationship with existence, and with football. Born in Algiers into relative poverty, he found consolation in the escapism provided by the footballing field, going on to represent the junior team of Racing Universitaire d’Alger (RUA) as a goalkeeper. The quote below — as well as this interview discussing his Nobel Prize, held at a live football match — is evidence of the importance that football had in shaping his thought.



For Camus, the simple morality seen on the footballing field — camaraderie and loyalty, for instance — contradicted the complicated moralities imposed by institutions like the state. Source


Perhaps, in addition to influencing his view on morality, the absurdity of the game also influenced Camus’s position on existence. He developed his thought to speak to the almost-central existentialist question — and probably the one most associated with the field of philosophy — on the meaning of existence: why are we even here? What does it mean to exist? Camus, in contrast to many others before him, posited that there can be no answer to this question. We shouldn’t look for any answers about purpose from our collective human exploits, or indeed from any aspect of the universe. Rather, the world does not follow the rational structure we expect it to. It is in this — the paradox that arises from our inevitable striving for meaning in the face of an ultimately unsympathetic and meaningless world — that Camus identifies the absurd.

“The absurd is born of this confrontation between the human need and the unreasonable silence of the world” — The Myth of Sisyphus

Camus’s understanding of absurdity is illustrated by an example from Greek mythology, the myth of Sisyphus. Without going into too much detail: Sisyphus was condemned by Zeus to strenuously roll a boulder to the top of a mountain, only to watch it roll back down again. Time and again. Forever. This seems quite tragic (not to mention tiring). However, Sisyphus knows what fate has given him: an eternity of toil and suffering with no clear goal or purpose. He knows that he is engaging in something ridiculous, something absurd, and that he cannot escape this bondage. It is in this self-knowledge where Camus sees an antidote to the absurd. Sisyphus, by accepting his fate and becoming conscious of it, becomes master over it. The acceptance of his fate affords him the possibility to shape his perception of it, and his perception can be one where life is full of vitality despite the constraints that his fate imposes on him. When the spectre of meaninglessness looks us in the eyes, the only remaining counter is to completely and unfailingly accept its gaze (and then invite him for a friendly game of footie).
Perhaps, we should accept that football — and indeed all other activities in life — is ultimately absurd; there is no purpose in kicking a round piece of leather around on a field of grass, and attempting to explain why it is meaningful is itself a ludicrous exercise. If, like Sisyphus, we acknowledge this meaninglessness and accept it, maybe we can create its importance anew. Maybe it is even possible to reimagine Sisyphus’s predicament. Instead of rolling a boulder up a hill, perhaps Sisyphus was kicking a ball about on a field all along, taking a shot at goal, missing, and then trudging after it. Maybe, in this situation, we should imagine that Sisyphus is as happy as I — and countless others — have been, doing this exact thing.



Sisyphus. Source



For more on this:

https://www.mmowen.me/camus-absurd-love-of-football — A blog I really enjoyed. It discusses Camus’s relationship with football on a deeper level than I attempted above.
Stanford Encyclopaedia of Philosophy — Camus

title: What Is Sonification?
text:
Sonification is the representation of data through sound — the audio equivalent of visualization. Just as a chart translates numbers into visual form, sonification translates them into acoustic form: pitch, rhythm, dynamics, timbre, all carrying information.


We think of data analysis as inherently visual. Charts, graphs, dashboards. But sound reveals patterns that silence obscures:

Temporal structure: Our ears are exquisitely tuned to detect rhythm and change over time. A rising tone tells you “this is increasing” faster than your eye can read coordinates.
Parallel information channels: A chart can show one or two dimensions clearly; sound can layer pitch, tempo, volume, and timbre simultaneously without visual clutter.
Emotional texture: Numbers are abstract. Sound is embodied — it creates feeling, context, memory. Data as music stays with you.
Accessibility: Not everyone can read graphs. Sound is a parallel language.




Sonification isn’t new:

Geiger counters: The iconic click-click-click of radiation detection. The sound is the measurement.
Medical sonification: Fetal heartbeat monitors, EEG audio representations — clinicians trained to hear abnormality before they see it.
Scientific research: Astronomers “listening” to pulsar signals; physicists sonifying particle detector data.
Market sentiment: Bloomberg terminals have had optional audio alerts for decades — a trader’s ear catches a price spike before the screen does.




Sonification isn’t just “play a sound when the data goes up.” Bad sonification is noise. Good sonification requires principled choices:

Mapping: Which data dimension maps to which sonic parameter?

Pitch naturally maps to numeric magnitude (higher = larger)
Tempo/rhythm maps to frequency or volatility (faster = more change)
Volume/dynamics maps to confidence, energy, or intensity
Timbre (instrument tone) can encode categorical information

Constraint: Unfettered audio chaos. Musical structure (scales, rhythm patterns, harmonic rules) creates intelligibility without sacrificing information.
Time: Sound unfolds in time. You cannot glance at a sonification. You must listen through. This means:

Compression matters (how much data per second?)
Narrative arc matters (does the piece have shape?)
Repetition works (patterns become clear on the second listen)

Honesty: Does the sonic representation reflect the data? Or does the music seduce you into misreading it?




Imagine daily temperature data for a week: [18, 20, 19, 22, 25, 23, 20]°C.
Simple sonification: - Scale: C major pentatonic (C, D, E, G, A) - Mapping: temperature → MIDI note (18°C → C3, 25°C → A4) - Rhythm: one beat per day, steady tempo - Duration: 4 seconds
Result: A short melodic phrase that rises (warming), dips (Friday cools), and falls (weekend). You hear the heatwave in its contour. The shape is the data.
More sophisticated mappings might add: - Volatility → velocity variation (swinging temperatures = staccato notes) - Magnitude → register shift (hot spell moves to higher register) - Trend → harmonic progression (stable = consonance, chaotic = dissonance)



Economic data tells stories that charts flatten. A yield curve’s shape matters — the market’s feeling about the future. A country’s exchange rate, its stock index, unemployment by region — these are numbers, yes, but they’re also signals of hope, fear, stress, opportunity.
This series translates three economic datasets into sound:

The yield curve — what bond markets expect for the future
Regional unemployment — the human cost of macroeconomic patterns, across provinces
Markets & currency — the crowd’s real-time sentiment about risk

Each post sonifies a different economic signal using the principles above. But first, you need to understand the language.







Hook: “Data is silent. But it doesn’t have to be.”
Definition: Sonification = data → sound
Why now? (relevance to this blog)





What does sound reveal that charts don’t?
Temporal perception, parallel channels, emotional impact
Accessibility angle





Geiger counter, medical sonification, astronomy, markets
Modern examples: data sonification in journalism, research, art





Mapping data → sonic parameters (pitch, tempo, volume, timbre)
Musical constraint as clarity, not limitation
Temporal structure: sound unfolds, you cannot glance
The ethics of representation: honesty in mapping





Temperature or stock price example (worked example with numbers)
Show the mapping table
Describe what you hear vs. what numbers show
Optional: Link to a 5–10 second audio clip of the sonified data (or generate one as an MP3)





Economics as emotion & story, not just numbers
Overview of the three posts to come
“Next: The yield curve” (teaser)





Invitation: Listen to the next piece with this framework in mind
Quote or reflection on sound + data







Tone: Accessible to non-technical readers, but not condescending. Assume they read news and care about data but may not know audio production.
Length: ~1,500–2,000 words (5–8 min read)
Media:

Consider 1–2 small diagrams (mapping table, simple waveform illustration)
Embedded audio clip(s) — can be generated from a simple Jupyter notebook as MP3s and placed in the post folder

Metadata: Tag as [sonification, methodology] or similar
Cross-links: Will be referenced in all three future posts as the foundational context





To make this concrete, you could: 1. Create a small Jupyter notebook that generates a 5–10 second sonification of public data (e.g., S&P 500 returns, ZAR/USD, temperature) 2. Export as MP3 to the blog post folder 3. Embed via Quarto’s audio syntax: ![](audio-file.mp3)
This turns the post from theory to proof-of-concept.

title: About
text:
I’m Hannes Coetsee, a South African t(h)inkerer, economist and data storyteller. I have experience in geospatial and remote sensing applications in central banking, time series analysis, and labour market research. My professional interests are often shaped by economic development, fairness and equality.
When I’m not thinkering, I enjoy hiking, philosophy, music (folk, latin and americana) and film. I also like cooking, although my skills are questionable.

title: About
text:
Looking for Expertise?
I work with organisations who need to make sense of data, whether that’s building an analysis from scratch, turning numbers into a story, or figuring out what the data is actually saying.
I’m available for:

Data analysis & modelling — economic, geospatial, labour market, time series
Data storytelling — turning complex findings into clear, compelling narratives
Research & writing — economic research, policy analysis, long-form writing

Get in touch

title: Hannes Coetsee
text:
The Value of Experience — Good Will Hunting and What it Means to ‘Know’


On Frank Jackson’s qualia, Mary’s Room, and what a park bench scene in Good Will Hunting tells us about the limits of knowledge.





September 6, 2021







Football, Absurdity and Camus


On the absurdity of football, Albert Camus, and why Sisyphus might have been kicking a ball around all along.
